title: Two Full Moons
content:
Weather From Dr. Nick’s Window: Second Full Moon Set to Close Out May 2026
The first full moon of May has already come and gone, lighting up the early part of the month. But the lunar cycle doesn’t stop there. Another full moon is on the way before May comes to an end, giving us a second full phase within the same calendar month.
This type of setup is uncommon, but not unheard of. The moon completes its cycle roughly every 29.5 days. Because most months are slightly longer than that, there are occasions when a full moon early in the month is followed by another near the end. When that happens, the second full moon is often referred to as a “Blue Moon.” Despite the name, there is no change in color—just timing.
The upcoming full moon will once again rise in the eastern sky around sunset and set in the west near sunrise, offering optimal viewing opportunities during the evening and early morning hours. As always, visibility will depend on cloud cover, which can vary widely this time of year across Missouri and Illinois with passing systems and late spring storm patterns.
Climatologically, late May often features a more active pattern across the mid-Mississippi Valley, with increasing humidity, stronger daytime heating, and the potential for scattered showers and thunderstorms. That means viewing conditions may change from night to night. Clear, calm evenings—often found behind departing cold fronts—will offer the best opportunity to take in the full moon in its entirety.
While the moon itself does not drive daily weather conditions such as storms or temperature swings, it remains an important part of the Earth’s overall system. Its gravitational influence plays a key role in ocean tides, particularly along coastal areas, where full and new moons often bring higher high tides and lower low tides due to the alignment of the Earth, moon, and sun. Even inland, the consistency of the lunar cycle provides a steady rhythm that contrasts with the variability of the atmosphere.
There are also subtle visual differences worth noting. A late-month full moon often appears slightly lower in the sky for parts of the night compared to winter full moons, due to the seasonal tilt of the Earth. This can give the moon a warmer hue near the horizon—shades of yellow, orange, or even a soft amber—caused by light scattering through a thicker layer of the atmosphere. It’s the same effect that gives us colorful sunsets.
For sky watchers, this second full moon provides another opportunity to observe a familiar but often overlooked feature of the night sky. Whether you’re catching it just above the horizon at dusk or high overhead later in the evening, it’s a reminder of the predictable mechanics happening above us—even when the weather below is anything but predictable.
In a month that often features active weather patterns, including thunderstorms and shifting temperatures, the return of a second full moon serves as a steady and reliable marker in the sky.
Clear skies permitting, it will be worth another look up.

title: What is PDS Tornado Watch
content:
Weather From Dr. Nick’s Window
What Is a PDS Tornado Watch?
Every now and then, a weather alert comes across that carries a little more weight than the rest. A PDS Tornado Watch is one of those alerts. It is not used often, and when it is, there is a reason behind it. It is the kind of headline that makes even seasoned meteorologists pause for a moment and take a closer look.
“PDS” stands for Particularly Dangerous Situation. That phrase is not chosen lightly. It is not there for effect or to grab attention. It is there to clearly communicate that the atmosphere can produce high-end severe weather. When the Storm Prediction Center issues a PDS Tornado Watch, it means forecasters have strong confidence that conditions support multiple tornadoes, including the potential for strong to violent tornadoes. These are the setups where storms can stay on the ground for long distances and cause significant damage in a short amount of time. It also signals that damaging winds and large hail may accompany the storms, adding to the overall risk.
These watches are issued only when several key ingredients come together in a very specific way. The atmosphere must have strong instability, which provides the fuel storms need to grow. There must be significant wind shear, which allows storms to rotate and organize. A boundary or front is usually in place to act as a trigger. And often, the environment favors supercell thunderstorms, which are the storms most capable of producing tornadoes. When all of these pieces align, confidence increases that the threat is not just possible, but likely.
Even with all of that, the decision to issue a PDS Tornado Watch is handled with care. Meteorologists do not rush to use that label. There is a level of responsibility that comes with it. If used too often, it loses its impact and people may begin to tune it out. If used too sparingly, the urgency of a truly dangerous situation may not come across when it should. That balance is something forecasters think about every time they evaluate a high-end severe weather setup.
At its core, a PDS Tornado Watch is about communication. It is a way of saying that this is not just another round of storms. It is a day where the atmosphere has the potential to produce something more serious. It is meant to stand out and prompt people to pay closer attention. Not out of fear, but out of awareness.
If one is issued for your area, it is a signal to be ready. Make sure you have multiple ways to receive warnings. Know where your safe place is ahead of time. Stay aware of changing weather conditions throughout the day. A watch means conditions are favorable, while a warning means action is needed. On a day like this, storms can develop and intensify quickly, so having a plan in place ahead of time matters.
In the end, a PDS Tornado Watch is not about creating panic. It is about giving you a clearer picture of what the atmosphere is capable of. It is about helping you stay one step ahead. And sometimes, that extra step is what makes all the difference.
